title: Beyond tools
text:
Chatting with your data (10 min)

A drop-in Shiny module that turns a data frame into a chat-driven explorer
The model writes the SQL or dplyr; the app runs it

title: Beyond tools
text:
Model Context Protocol (10 min)

A common standard for exposing tools and resources to LLM clients
Why this matters: write a server once, use it from many clients
MCP support is growing in Positron, VS Code, Claude Desktop, etc.

title: Beyond tools
text:
Activity (18-mcp)

Connect an MCP server to ellmer or chatlas
Options to try: ArXiV, fetcher, git-ingest, github, context7
Notice: the model now has access to tools you did not write

title: Beyond tools
text:
What is an agent? (30 min)

Hadley / Simon Willison short definition: an LLM with a read tool and a write tool
The “you know it when you see it” definition: autonomous, long-context, minimal intervention
Demo: a working agent (databot-style) doing a real task end to end

title: Beyond tools
text:
Hopes and fears (30 min)

Where these tools seem to be going
What to watch out for: cost, lock-in, evaluation, trust
Open discussion: what does this change about your day-to-day work?

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
The landscape (15 min)

Major providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, plus local options like ollama
Tradeoffs: capability, context length, speed, cost
Newer is not always better for your task

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activity: same question, different model (05-models)

Change one string to switch providers: chat_openai() vs. chat_anthropic(), etc.
Compare answers, latency, and token usage

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Beyond text (15 min)

Vision: pass an image alongside your prompt
PDFs: pass document bytes directly
When to extract text up front vs. let the model see the file

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activities

06-vision: pass food photos and ask for recipe suggestions
07-pdf: take a PDF recipe and turn it into clean Markdown

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Getting parseable answers (15 min)

R: ellmer::type_*()
Python: Pydantic models with chatlas
Schemas double as documentation for the model

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activity (08-structured-output)

Extract structured fields from the recipe PDF: ingredients, steps, yield, prep time
Tip: turn on use_attribute_docstrings in Pydantic to let docstrings guide the model

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Scaling out (10 min)

When one prompt becomes a hundred
Parallel API calls vs. provider batch APIs (cheaper, slower)
Watch for rate limits

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activity (09-batch)

Re-run the structured extraction across many recipes in parallel
Save the JSON output so we can reuse it later

title: Programming with LLMs
section: Models will confidently make things up (35 min)
text:
Models will confidently make things up (35 min)

What hallucination looks like
Why a confident answer is not a correct answer

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activity: ask about a plot (10-plot-interpret)

Generate a plot of mpg vs. weight from mtcars
Ask the model to interpret it
Discuss what it got right and where it leaned on priors

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
Activity: ask about noise (11-plot-noise)

Replace the plot with random noise, keep the prompt the same
What does the model say?
Part 2: pair up and try to steer the model into a “decent” interpretation of pure noise

title: Programming with LLMs
text:
System prompt best practices

Be explicit about role, audience, and format
Show, don’t just tell (few-shot examples)
Tell the model how to handle uncertainty

title: Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners
text:
Welcome
These are the materials for a two-day workshop at the 50th Summer Institute of Applied Statistics (SIAS) at Brigham Young University on 17-18 June 2026, taught by Julia Silge. The workshop is split into two days:

Day 1, Build on AI: programming with large language models (LLMs) from R and Python using ellmer and chatlas, covering prompts, token management, structured output, tool calling, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and Shiny chat interfaces.
Day 2, Build with AI: using AI coding assistants effectively as a data practitioner, from inline completions to chat-based help to agentic, more autonomous workflows.

No prior AI background is required; the workshop is built for both newcomers and experienced data practitioners.

title: Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners
text:
Learning objectives
At the end of this workshop, participants will understand how to:

Call LLMs from R and Python using ellmer and/or chatlas.
Design effective system prompts and reason about token usage, history, and cost.
Use structured output and tool calling to make LLMs reliably interact with data and other systems.
Build retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workflows and wrap LLM-powered features into Shiny apps.
Choose between inline, chat, and agentic AI coding assistants and use each one effectively.
Maintain control of AI-assisted code through good context, tests, and plans.

title: Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners
text:
Preparation
We will be working locally during the workshop, so please come with a laptop that has the following installed (all available for free):

A recent version of Python (perhaps installed using uv) and/or R
A recent version of your IDE of choice, such as Positron, RStudio, or VS Code
Clone the workshop repo
Install the Python packages from requirements.txt and/or the R packages via TODO.

You will also need API credentials for at least one LLM provider; we will share details closer to the workshop.

title: Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners
text:
Instructor bio
Julia Silge is a data scientist and engineering manager at Posit PBC, where she works on open source software for data science in Python and R. She is an author, an international keynote speaker, and a real-world practitioner focusing on data analysis and machine learning. Julia loves text analysis, making beautiful charts, and communicating about technical topics with diverse audiences.

title: Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners
text:
Credits
Illustrations throughout the workshop materials are from unDraw and used under their license.

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
Who are you?

You know some R and/or Python
You are curious about working with LLMs from code
You want hands-on practice, not just slides

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
Day 1: Build on AI

Talking with LLMs via code (this session)
Programming with LLMs
Augmented generation
Beyond tools

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
Welcome (10 min)

Introductions
What to expect from these two days

title: Talking with LLMs via code
section: Set-up and verify API access (10 min)
text:
Set-up and verify API access (10 min)

How API keys work
Where to put them so the workshop code can find them

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
Think empirically, be pragmatic (10 min)

The right mindset for working with LLMs
Models are not deterministic; try things, iterate, compare
Extra time here to troubleshoot setup as needed

title: Talking with LLMs via code
section: To get a response, you send a message (20 min)
text:
To get a response, you send a message (20 min)

Under the hood: an HTTP request to a provider
Message roles: system, user, assistant
ellmer and chatlas hide the boilerplate

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
Activity: word guessing game (02-word-game)

System prompt: You are playing a word guessing game. At each turn, guess the word and tell us what it is.
First message includes a modifier (e.g. “In British English,” “In pirate,”) that steers later answers

title: Talking with LLMs via code
text:
The conversation is stateless

The model has no memory between requests
The library replays history each turn
Demo (clearbot): show what happens when you clear the chat

title: Talking with LLMs via code
section: Tokens as the fundamental unit (20 min)
text:
Tokens as the fundamental unit (20 min)

What is a token?
How models predict the next token
Why this matters for cost and context length
Demo: token possibilities visualizer

title: Talking with LLMs via code
section: Try a chat UI in seconds (20 min)
text:
Try a chat UI in seconds (20 min)

live_console() / live_browser() in ellmer
chat.console() / chat.app() in chatlas

title: Talking with LLMs via code
section: Build your own Shiny chat app
text:
Build your own Shiny chat app

Shiny chat_ui() and chat_append() (R: also chat_mod_server())

title: Augmented generation
section: Context in, better answers out (10 min)
text:
Context in, better answers out (10 min)

“Retrieval-Augmented Generation” without any fancy infrastructure
Stuff the relevant docs into the prompt yourself
Most “RAG” is this idea plus automation

title: Augmented generation
text:
Activity: coding assistant (13-coding-assistant)

Ask the LLM to write a function using a niche package (R: weathR, Python: NWS)
First with no context; note what goes wrong
Then paste the README into the prompt and compare

title: Augmented generation
text:
How RAG actually works (30 min)

Chunk your documents
Compute embeddings for each chunk
At query time, find the most similar chunks and add them to the prompt
The “retrieval” is just nearest-neighbor search

title: Augmented generation
section: Activity: build a dynamic RAG system (14-rag)
text:
Activity: build a dynamic RAG system (14-rag)

Source documents: R for Data Science (R) or Polars Cookbook (Python)
Tools: ragnar (R), llama-index or chatlas helpers (Python)
Wire retrieval up as a tool the model can call

title: Augmented generation
section: Letting the model use your functions (20 min)
text:
Letting the model use your functions (20 min)

The model decides when to call; you decide what is callable
The shape: function name, description, JSON schema for arguments
You execute the function and send the result back

title: Augmented generation
text:
Demos

Manual loop (_demos/manual-tools): see every step
Automatic loop (_demos/tools): let the library handle the round trips

title: Augmented generation
text:
Activity: quiz show with tools (15-quiz-game-tools)

Provide a function that plays a sound (R: beepr, Python: playsound)
Document it well and register it as a tool in the quiz show app
Watch the model learn when to play which sound

title: Augmented generation
section: Showing tool activity to users (30 min)
text:
Showing tool activity to users (30 min)

Tool annotations can give each tool an icon and title
ContentToolResult can return a custom title and icon
Tool calls can update reactive state in your Shiny app
